title: Available User Seats and Active User Tracking
story: As a product administrator, I want to see my available user seat limit and active user count, so that I can manage my team's license allocation and understand my current capacity.
description:
Display and manage the relationship between available user seats (controlled by subscription) and active users (controlled by admin actions in DataTools).

The account management page shows 'Available User Limit' and 'Active Users' counts. Disabling a user decreases the active count. The seat limit is controlled by the subscription (updated via API from Stripe). The invitation middleware was updated to only count active users against the limit, ignoring pending invitations and disabled users.

For downgrades, a nightly cron job enforces seat limits by restricting admin access when active users exceed available seats, forcing admins to disable users or purchase more licenses rather than automatically disabling end users.
acceptance_criteria: Account page displays 'Available User Limit' and 'Active Users' counts
Disabling a user decreases the active user count
Invitation middleware only counts active users against the seat limit
Admins cannot enable more users than their available seat limit
Nightly cron job enforces seat limits by restricting admin access when over-utilized
End users are never automatically disabled by the system
ux_changes: Account page shows Available User Limit and Active Users. Enable/disable toggle for team members with visual indicators.
middleware_changes: Invitation middleware updated to count only active users. Nightly cron job enforces seat limits. Admins are restricted rather than end users when over-utilized.
data_model_changes: Status field added to the team-user intermediate table for enable/disable state instead of hard deleting records.
api_changes: API endpoint for updating available seat count from subscription system (Stripe).
implementation_notes: Key design decision: never automatically disable end users. Instead, restrict admin operations when over-utilized. This follows industry best practice where the admin manages license allocation, not the system. The cron runs nightly to check for downgrades based on date stamps.
comment_summary: Developer implemented enable/disable for team accounts with middleware updates. Product provided detailed downgrade rules: restrict admins not end users, nightly cron enforcement, no automatic user disabling. Email templates needed for enable/disable/downgrade notifications.
